skill.name: review
skill.description: Review the changes since a fixed point (commit, branch, tag, or merge-base) along two axes — Standards (does the code follow this repo's documented coding standards?) and Spec (does the code match what the originating issue/PRD asked for?). Runs both reviews in parallel sub-agents and reports them side by side. Use when the user wants to review a branch, a PR, work-in-progress changes, or asks to "review since X".
skill.summary: Two-axis code review (Standards + Spec) of diffs since a fixed point, run in parallel sub-agents.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static scanner flagged 37 potential issues, all of which are false positives on review. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' matches are Markdown inline-code formatting (e.g. `HEAD`, `git diff`, `CLAUDE.md`), not actual shell execution. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'system reconnaissance' hits on the description line and fixed-point line are also false positives — the skill documents a git-based review workflow with no crypto or reconnaissance operations. The only legitimate risk factor is `external_commands` because the skill instructs running `git diff` and `git log`, which is expected and safe for a code review skill.

medium_findings.title: Git command execution is part of the skill's core workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs running `git diff <fixed-point>...HEAD` and `git log <fixed-point>..HEAD --oneline` against a user-supplied fixed point. The fixed point is passed through verbatim from the user, which is the intended behavior for a review tool, but reviewers should be aware that arbitrary branch/SHA names will be interpolated into shell-style commands. This is acceptable for a code review skill but warrants a low-risk note.

low_findings.title: Static scanner false positive: Markdown backticks flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: The scanner reported 35 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings across SKILL.md. Manual review confirms every match is a Markdown inline-code span (e.g. `HEAD`, `git diff`, `CLAUDE.md`, `general-purpose`, `## Standards`). No actual shell command construction via backticks is present in the file.

low_findings.title: Static scanner false positive: 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' on description line
low_findings.description: The scanner reported a 'weak cryptographic algorithm' pattern on the frontmatter description line. Manual review shows the line contains no cryptographic primitives, algorithm names, or hash references — only natural-language description text. Dismissed as a false positive.

low_findings.title: Static scanner false positive: 'System reconnaissance' on fixed-point line
low_findings.description: The scanner reported 'system reconnaissance' on the line that says 'Whatever the user said is the fixed point — a commit SHA, branch name, tag, `main`, `HEAD~5`, etc.' This is the skill explaining how to identify a git reference, not a reconnaissance operation. Dismissed as a false positive.

content.user_title: Review branch diffs against both repo standards and the original spec
content.value_statement: Reviewing a branch by eye misses half the picture — code can pass the style guide and still implement the wrong thing. This skill runs Standards and Spec reviews in parallel sub-agents and reports them side by side so neither axis can mask the other.
actual_capabilities: Reviews the diff between any fixed point (commit, branch, tag, merge-base) and HEAD using `git diff` and `git log`.
Runs two parallel sub-agents — one for Standards (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, ADRs, lint configs) and one for Spec (issue tracker, PRD files, user-supplied spec path).
Identifies and reads the originating spec from commit-message references, user-supplied paths, or repo doc directories (`docs/`, `specs/`, `.scratch/`).
Asks the user for a fixed point and a spec source when they are not provided, instead of guessing.
Presents Standards and Spec findings as two independent sections so the user can see each axis without merging or reranking.
Skips the Spec sub-agent and reports 'no spec available' when no spec source can be located.
limitations: Does not merge or rerank findings across the two axes — by design, so neither axis masks the other.
Assumes the issue tracker workflow file `docs/agents/issue-tracker.md` is present, or that `/setup-matt-pocock-skills` has been run to create it.
Will not proceed without a user-supplied fixed point — it always asks first instead of defaulting to `main` or `HEAD~1`.
Re-checks only standards that lint and formatter tooling does not already enforce; the standards sub-agent skips machine-enforced rules.

use_cases.title: PR review before merge
use_cases.description: An engineer finishing a feature branch runs the skill to verify the diff matches the originating issue and respects the repo's documented coding standards before opening the PR.
use_cases.target_user: Software engineers preparing a pull request

use_cases.title: Mid-task sanity check on work-in-progress
use_cases.description: A developer midway through a multi-commit change runs the skill to check that the work so far stays aligned with the spec and the project's standards before continuing.

use_cases.title: Tech-lead audit of an unowned branch
use_cases.description: A tech lead reviewing a colleague's branch uses the skill to surface spec deviations and standards violations in one pass, with citations to the specific files and rules.
use_cases.target_user: Tech leads and senior engineers auditing other people's work

prompt_templates.title: Review a feature branch against the spec
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the changes on the branch `feature/checkout-flow` against `main`. The originating issue is #482 in our tracker.
prompt_templates.scenario: Standard end-to-end review with a known spec source

prompt_templates.title: Review work-in-progress against the PRD
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my work-in-progress since `HEAD~5` against the spec in `docs/prds/billing-redesign.md`. I have not opened a PR yet.
prompt_templates.scenario: Mid-task review with an explicit local PRD path

prompt_templates.title: Review a specific commit
prompt_templates.prompt: Review just the changes introduced by commit `a1b2c3d4` against the spec at `specs/onboarding-v2.md`. I want a Standards + Spec breakdown.
prompt_templates.scenario: Narrow review of a single commit

prompt_templates.title: Review with no fixed point yet
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to review my recent work but I am not sure what to compare against. Ask me which fixed point I want and which spec to use, then run the two-axis review.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt — let the skill drive the discovery

output_examples.input: Review the changes on `feature/checkout-flow` against `main`. The originating issue is #482.
output: - `src/checkout/CheckoutForm.tsx` — uses inline `style` props; the project standard in `CONTRIBUTING.md` requires `css-modules` for component styling.
- `src/checkout/api.ts` — handler returns `Promise<any>`; `tsconfig.json` `strict: true` is on, so this should be `Promise<CheckoutResult>`.
- (2 minor judgement calls: prop ordering, comment tone — see sub-agent report.)
- Missing: spec asked for guest-checkout support (#482, acceptance criterion 3) — no guest-checkout branch in the diff.
- Partial: spec asked for an Apple Pay option; code adds `paymentMethods: ['card']` only.
- Scope creep: PR also refactors the cart store, which was not in the issue.
Summary: 2 hard Standards violations, 2 missing spec items, 1 scope-creep item. Worst: missing guest-checkout support.
- `src/billing/plan.ts` — exports a default object; project standard in `AGENTS.md` is named exports only.
- (Tooling-enforced rules such as `eslint` formatting were skipped per skill instructions.)
- Implemented: proration on plan upgrade, downgrade preview, invoice line-item breakdown — all match PRD sections 4.1–4.3.
- Missing: PRD section 4.4 (tax recalculation on mid-cycle change) is not in the diff.
- Looks wrong: `proratedAmount` in `plan.ts:42` uses calendar days instead of billing-cycle days; PRD example uses billing-cycle days.
Summary: 1 Standards finding, 2 Spec findings (1 missing, 1 likely bug). Worst: tax recalculation not implemented.

best_practices: Always provide a specific fixed point (branch, commit SHA, or tag). Vague references like 'recent work' force the skill to ask before proceeding.
Point the skill at the originating spec up front — a path, a tracker issue, or a PRD file — so the Spec sub-agent has something concrete to compare against.
Keep the two reports separate. Do not collapse Standards and Spec into a single ranked list; each axis is meant to be read on its own.
anti_patterns: Do not default the fixed point to `main` or `HEAD~1` — if the user has not given one, ask. Guessing produces reviews of the wrong slice of history.
Do not merge the Standards and Spec sub-agent outputs into a single ranking. The two axes can contradict each other, and ranking hides that.
Do not re-check rules that lint or formatter tooling already enforces. The Standards sub-agent should skip machine-enforced rules by design.

faq.question: What is the difference between the Standards and Spec axes?
faq.answer: Standards checks whether the code follows the repo's documented coding rules. Spec checks whether the code actually implements what the originating issue or PRD asked for. A change can pass one and fail the other, which is exactly why this skill reports them separately.

faq.question: Do I need to give a fixed point?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill will not guess. If you do not specify one, it will ask you to choose a branch, commit, tag, or merge-base before proceeding.

faq.question: What if there is no spec for the change?
faq.answer: The Spec sub-agent will skip and the final report will say 'no spec available'. The Standards review will still run normally.

faq.question: Which sub-agents does the skill use?
faq.answer: Both Standards and Spec sub-agents use the `general-purpose` subagent. They are launched in a single message with two `Agent` tool calls so they run in parallel.

faq.question: Will the skill re-run lint or format checks?
faq.answer: No. Machine-enforced standards such as ESLint, Prettier, Biome, and tsconfig rules are explicitly skipped by the Standards sub-agent to avoid duplicate work.

faq.question: How long is the output?
faq.answer: Each sub-agent is asked to stay under 400 words. The final aggregated report adds a one-line summary with totals and the worst single finding per axis.
